news_summary: Recent advancements in human rights highlight groundbreaking archaeological discoveries, innovative housing solutions for underserved communities, legal protections for activists, and improved support for families with newborns.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes en matière de droits de l'homme mettent en lumière des découvertes archéologiques révolutionnaires, des solutions innovantes en matière de logement pour les communautés défavorisées, des protections juridiques pour les militants et un soutien accru aux familles avec des nouveau-nés.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve descubrimientos arqueológicos revolucionarios, soluciones innovadoras de vivienda para comunidades desfavorecidas, protecciones legales para activistas y un mayor apoyo a las familias con recién nacidos.

articles.title: Lost for 150,000 years: Rainforest discovery upends human history
articles.summary: For decades, scientists believed ancient humans avoided dense rainforests, treating them as nearly impossible environments for early survival. But a groundbreaking discovery in West Africa is rewriting that story. Researchers uncovered evidence that humans were living deep within rainforest environments in present-day Côte d'Ivoire around 150,000 years ago — far earlier than anyone thought possible.
articles.content:
Dense tropical rainforests were long considered some of the last places early humans could survive. For decades, researchers believed our ancestors mainly stuck to open grasslands and coastal regions, avoiding the thick forests of Africa until much later in history. Evidence from West Africa is now forcing scientists to rethink that assumption in a dramatic way.

Researchers investigating an archaeological site in present-day Côte d'Ivoire found evidence that humans were living in wet tropical forests roughly 150,000 years ago. The discovery pushes back the oldest known evidence of rainforest habitation by more than double previous estimates and suggests early Homo sapiens were far more adaptable than once believed.

The findings, published in Nature, support a growing view that human evolution did not happen in one single environment. Instead, ancient populations appear to have thrived across a surprising range of ecosystems, from deserts and coastlines to dense forests.

Ancient Stone Tools Hidden Beneath the Forest

The story began decades ago. In the 1980s, Professor Yodé Guédé of l'Université Félix Houphouët-Boigny helped investigate a site known as Bété I during a joint Ivorian-Soviet research mission. Excavations uncovered layers of stone tools buried deep underground in what is now rainforest territory.

At the time, researchers could not accurately determine how old the tools were or what the environment looked like when ancient humans lived there. That changed when an international team returned to the site using modern technology unavailable to scientists forty years earlier.

"With Professor Guédé's help, we relocated the original trench and were able to re-investigate it using state of the art methods that were not available thirty to forty years ago," said Dr. James Blinkhorn of the University of Liverpool and the Max Planck Institute of Geoanthropology.

The timing turned out to be critical. Since the new excavation, mining activity has destroyed the site, making the recovered data especially valuable.

Evidence of a True Rainforest Environment

To determine the age of the site, scientists used multiple dating techniques, including Optically Stimulated Luminescence and Electron-Spin Resonance. Both methods pointed to human occupation around 150,000 years ago.

Researchers then analyzed pollen, phytoliths (tiny silica structures left behind by plants), and chemical traces preserved in sediments. The results showed the area was heavily forested at the time humans lived there.

The samples contained pollen and plant waxes associated with humid West African rainforests, while very low levels of grass pollen suggested the site was surrounded by dense woodland rather than a thin strip of forest.

Before this discovery, the oldest secure evidence of humans living in African rainforests dated to only about 18,000 years ago. The previous global record for rainforest habitation came from Southeast Asia and dated to around 70,000 years ago.

"Before our study, the oldest secure evidence for habitation in African rainforests was around 18 thousand years ago and the oldest evidence of rainforest habitation anywhere came from southeast Asia at about 70 thousand years ago," explained lead author Dr. Eslem Ben Arous. "This pushes back the oldest known evidence of humans in rainforests by more than double the previously known estimate."

Rethinking Human Evolution

The discovery adds to a growing body of evidence showing that early humans were ecological generalists capable of surviving in many different habitats. Scientists increasingly believe that this flexibility may have helped Homo sapiens spread successfully across the world while other human relatives disappeared.

Follow-up discussions surrounding the research have also highlighted how difficult rainforest archaeology can be. Fossils rarely survive in hot, humid environments, and dense vegetation makes excavations challenging. Because of this, many scientists suspect there could be far older rainforest sites still waiting to be found across Africa.

The study also raises bigger questions about how long humans have influenced tropical ecosystems. Researchers are now exploring whether ancient populations may have shaped rainforest environments far earlier than previously assumed through hunting, fire use, and plant management.

"Convergent evidence shows beyond doubt that ecological diversity sits at the heart of our species," said Professor Eleanor Scerri, senior author of the study. "This reflects a complex history of population subdivision, in which different populations lived in different regions and habitat types."

Scientists believe the Côte d'Ivoire discovery may only be the beginning. Several additional sites in the region remain largely unexplored, raising the possibility that even older evidence of rainforest-dwelling humans could still be uncovered.

The research was funded by the Max Planck Society and the Leakey Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The discovery provides groundbreaking evidence that early humans inhabited dense rainforests 150,000 years ago, significantly revising human evolutionary history and demonstrating early human adaptability across diverse environments. This has broad scientific significance and enriches our understanding of human origins, with tangible impact on anthropology and archaeology.
articles.personality_title: Ancient humans lived in African rainforests 150,000 years ago, new discovery shows
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists long thought early humans avoided thick rainforests, believing these environments were too tough for survival. They assumed our ancestors mostly lived in open grasslands or near coasts until much later in history.

**What happened** – Researchers found evidence in Côte d'Ivoire, West Africa, showing humans lived deep in rainforests about 150,000 years ago. This was discovered by studying stone tools and plant remains at a site called Bété I, using modern dating methods and analysis.

**Impact** – This finding doubles the previous oldest known date for humans living in rainforests and shows early humans were more adaptable than thought. It changes how scientists see human evolution, proving our ancestors lived in many environments, not just open spaces.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to explore more sites in the region to find even older evidence. They also want to study how early humans might have affected rainforest ecosystems through activities like hunting and managing plants.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New evidence reveals that early humans lived in West African rainforests 150,000 years ago, showing they adapted to diverse environments much earlier than believed.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des humains anciens vivaient dans les forêts tropicales africaines il y a 150 000 ans, révèle une nouvelle découverte
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques pensaient que les premiers humains évitaient les forêts tropicales denses, estimant que ces environnements étaient trop difficiles pour survivre. Ils croyaient que nos ancêtres vivaient surtout dans les savanes ouvertes ou près des côtes, et ce, bien plus tard dans l’histoire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé des preuves en Côte d'Ivoire, en Afrique de l’Ouest, montrant que des humains vivaient profondément dans les forêts tropicales il y a environ 150 000 ans. Ils ont étudié des outils en pierre et des restes de plantes sur un site nommé Bété I, en utilisant des méthodes modernes de datation et d’analyse.

**Impact** – Cette découverte double l’âge connu le plus ancien de la présence humaine dans les forêts tropicales et montre que les premiers humains étaient plus adaptables qu’on ne le pensait. Elle change la vision de l’évolution humaine, prouvant que nos ancêtres vivaient dans plusieurs types d’environnements, pas seulement en plein air.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont explorer d’autres sites dans la région pour trouver des preuves encore plus anciennes. Ils veulent aussi étudier comment les premiers humains ont pu influencer les écosystèmes des forêts par la chasse et la gestion des plantes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles preuves montrent que les premiers humains vivaient dans les forêts tropicales d’Afrique de l’Ouest il y a 150 000 ans, révélant une adaptation précoce à des environnements variés.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos hallazgos muestran que humanos antiguos vivieron en selvas africanas hace 150,000 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos pensaron que los primeros humanos evitaban las selvas densas porque eran ambientes difíciles para sobrevivir. Creían que nuestros antepasados vivían principalmente en pastizales abiertos o cerca de las costas, y solo mucho después en la historia entraron en las selvas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron pruebas en Costa de Marfil, África Occidental, de que humanos vivían en selvas tropicales hace unos 150,000 años. Esto se descubrió al estudiar herramientas de piedra y restos de plantas en un sitio llamado Bété I, usando métodos modernos para datar y analizar.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo duplica la fecha más antigua conocida de humanos viviendo en selvas y muestra que los primeros humanos eran más adaptables de lo que se pensaba. Cambia la forma en que los científicos entienden la evolución humana, demostrando que vivían en muchos ambientes diferentes, no solo en espacios abiertos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean explorar más sitios en la región para encontrar pruebas aún más antiguas. También quieren estudiar cómo los humanos antiguos pudieron haber influido en los ecosistemas de la selva mediante la caza y el manejo de plantas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nuevas pruebas revelan que humanos antiguos vivieron en las selvas de África Occidental hace 150,000 años, mostrando una adaptación temprana a ambientes diversos.
articles.image_prompt: An ancient stone tool partially buried in rich, dark soil at the edge of a vibrant, dense West African rainforest, with layers of pollen and plant wax patterns subtly woven into the earth beneath, all bathed in warm, golden light that suggests both deep history and hopeful discovery.

articles.title: From favelas to informal settlements, new UN report points to housing solutions that work
articles.summary: From Thailand to Jordan, from Brazil to Germany, new approaches to housing are quietly taking shape. Residents of informal settlements once facing eviction are rebuilding their communities with State support. Refugees and host communities are reclaiming neglected spaces, turning them greener and safer. In Brazil, favelas are being upgraded rather than torn down, while in Germany, rent controls are helping to steady the market.
articles.content:
A major new UN report says such efforts are more than isolated successes. With the right tools, it argues, easing the global housing crisis – impacting billions – may be within reach.

Released by UN-Habitat and launched on Tuesday at the 13th World Urban Forum (WUF13), in Baku, Azerbaijan, the report also points to a broader role for the UN system in helping countries move beyond short-term fixes towards long-term housing solutions rooted in human rights, climate resilience and community participation.

Tweet URL

Held every two years, the forum brings together policymakers, practitioners and community leaders, offering a space to connect local experience with global decision-making – from slum upgrading and affordable housing finance to climate adaptation and post-conflict reconstruction.

The World Cities Report 2026: The Global Housing Crisis – Pathways to Action paints a stark picture.

Up to 3.4 billion people worldwide lack access to adequate housing, while more than 1.1 billion live in informal settlements and slums. Yet across its 300-plus pages, the report emphasises not only the scale of the challenge but also examples of what works.

UN as coordinator, advocate and partner

UN-Habitat says the role of the United Nations is not simply to sound the alarm, but to help governments, cities and communities build practical solutions.

The report describes housing as central to sustainable development and calls for greater political priority through the New Urban Agenda, an action-oriented framework adopted in 2016 that sets global standards for urban planning and helps advance the urban dimensions of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

Housing should be seen as more than a market commodity, says UN-Habitat Executive Director Anacláudia Rossbach, “Adequate housing represents one of the most powerful entry points for accelerating sustainable and inclusive development.”

The UN’s role includes:

helping governments develop housing policies,

promoting housing as a human right,

coordinating international cooperation,

supporting climate-resilient urban planning,

backing community-led upgrading projects.

Rather than top-down solutions, the report emphasizes partnership with local communities, illustrating the approach through case studies from different regions.

UN Climate Change/Habib Samadov

Thailand: community-driven upgrading

One of the flagship examples is Thailand’s Baan Mankong programme, widely seen as a model for participatory housing development. Instead of relocating residents from informal settlements, it provides infrastructure funding and supports collective land agreements, allowing communities to improve housing in place.

UN-Habitat presents this as evidence that informal settlements should not automatically be viewed as failures of urban development. At the same time, the report notes challenges: reliance on community savings groups can lead to uneven progress, with poorer communities struggling to meet requirements.

Jordan: inclusive urban spaces

In Amman, interventions have included the rehabilitation of a large open space near the Al-Hussein refugee camp into a climate-resilient, age-responsive park.

Such projects aim to ease tensions between displaced people and host communities while improving living conditions for all, with attention to the needs of women and girls. The report urges cities to treat displaced populations not as temporary outsiders, but as urban residents entitled to services, jobs and safe housing.

It also situates the example within a broader global context: by the end of 2024, more than 123 million people had been forcibly displaced by conflict, violence and persecution, alongside millions uprooted by disasters. In this context, the UN sees its role as bridging the gap between humanitarian response and long-term urban development.

© Unsplash/Nerea Martí Sesarin

Brazil: upgrading instead of eviction

Brazil’s favela programmes illustrate a shift away from eviction and slum clearance, policies the report says often deepened poverty and social exclusion.

Instead, UN-Habitat promotes “in situ upgrading” – improving roads, sanitation, drainage and housing conditions without displacing residents. The approach allows solutions to be tailored to each area, from housing improvements in São Paulo to drainage projects in Recife and the construction of a cable car in Rio de Janeiro’s Complexo do Alemão.

Climate-resilient housing

The report places housing at the centre of the climate crisis. Buildings account for around 37 per cent of global greenhouse gas emissions, while climate-related hazards could destroy 167 million homes by 2040. In 2023 alone, natural disasters caused $280 billion in economic losses, most of them uninsured.

UN-Habitat argues that climate-resilient housing must become a global priority. Examples highlighted in the report include community-led upgrading projects in Cambodia and innovative governance initiatives in the Philippines, where residents collectively plan and build their homes.

In Tanzania, rapid electrification has more than doubled access – from 15 per cent in 2020 to 40 per cent in 2022 – providing an alternative to polluting fuels such as charcoal and opening the way for cleaner practices like eCooking. To support the transition, the national electricity provider has introduced financing schemes for appliances, while project partners have developed recipe guides tailored to electric cooking.

The report also warns that climate adaptation must not come at the expense of low-income communities through forced relocation or “green gentrification.”

© UN-Habitat/Matthijs van Oostrum

Housing as a human right

The UN frames housing not only as an economic issue, but as a matter of human rights. The report calls on governments to strengthen protections against forced evictions, recognize diverse forms of land tenure and involve communities more closely in decision-making.

Even in developed countries, segregation remains entrenched. In Europe, many first- and second-generation immigrants remain concentrated in low-income neighbourhoods, while in the United States housing segregation increased in most cities between 1990 and 2014, a divide laid bare again during the COVID-19 pandemic.

The report argues that housing policy must move beyond narrow models of homeownership to include rental housing, cooperatives and community-led approaches. For UN-Habitat, there is no single global solution; progress depends on cooperation between governments, international organizations and residents themselves.

As Executive Director Anacláudia Rossbach writes in the foreword, “the actions we take now will determine whether housing becomes a foundation for stability and growth, or a source of acute vulnerability.”

UN News is in Baku covering the Summit throughout the week. Follow along here.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a major UN report highlighting effective, scalable housing solutions addressing the global housing crisis impacting billions. It emphasizes housing as a human right, showcases diverse successful community-driven projects worldwide, and links housing improvements to climate resilience and social inclusion. The story has broad significance and substance, focusing on sustainable, inclusive development and long-term global impact.
articles.personality_title: UN report highlights successful global housing solutions improving lives and communities
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Around the world, billions of people lack decent homes. Many live in informal settlements or slums, facing poor conditions and insecurity. The United Nations released a new report at a global urban forum to explore ways to fix this housing crisis.

**What happened** – The UN-Habitat report, launched at the 13th World Urban Forum in Baku, Azerbaijan, shares examples from countries like Thailand, Brazil, Jordan, Germany, and Tanzania. These examples show how communities and governments are working together to improve housing without forcing people to move. Projects include upgrading favelas in Brazil, creating climate-friendly parks in Jordan, and supporting community-led housing improvements in Thailand.

**Impact** – This report is important because it shows practical solutions that work across different places and cultures. It also stresses that housing is a human right, not just something bought or sold. The report links better housing to fighting climate change and helping vulnerable groups like refugees. It challenges old ideas of eviction and slum clearance, focusing instead on upgrading homes where people already live.

**What's next step** – The UN wants governments and cities to use these examples to create long-term housing plans based on human rights and climate resilience. It encourages more cooperation between local communities and international groups to make housing safe, affordable, and inclusive for everyone.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN report shows that by working together with communities, countries can create housing solutions that improve lives while respecting people’s rights and the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le rapport de l’ONU met en lumière des solutions réussies pour le logement qui améliorent les vies et les communautés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans le monde, des milliards de personnes n’ont pas de logement décent. Beaucoup vivent dans des quartiers informels ou des bidonvilles, dans des conditions difficiles et sans sécurité. L’ONU a publié un nouveau rapport lors d’un forum mondial sur l’urbanisme pour explorer des solutions à cette crise du logement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le rapport d’ONU-Habitat, présenté au 13e Forum mondial sur la ville à Bakou, en Azerbaïdjan, partage des exemples de pays comme la Thaïlande, le Brésil, la Jordanie, l’Allemagne et la Tanzanie. Ces exemples montrent comment les communautés et les gouvernements travaillent ensemble pour améliorer le logement sans déplacer les habitants. Des projets incluent la rénovation des favelas au Brésil, la création de parcs adaptés au climat en Jordanie, et le soutien à l’amélioration participative du logement en Thaïlande.

**Impact** – Ce rapport est important car il présente des solutions pratiques qui fonctionnent dans différents pays et cultures. Il souligne aussi que le logement est un droit humain, pas seulement une marchandise. Le rapport relie l’amélioration du logement à la lutte contre le changement climatique et à l’aide aux personnes vulnérables comme les réfugiés. Il remet en cause les anciennes idées d’éviction et de démolition des bidonvilles, en privilégiant la rénovation sur place.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU encourage les gouvernements et les villes à utiliser ces exemples pour créer des plans de logement durables fondés sur les droits humains et la résilience climatique. Elle incite à renforcer la coopération entre les communautés locales et les organisations internationales pour rendre le logement sûr, abordable et inclusif.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le rapport de l’ONU montre qu’en travaillant avec les communautés, les pays peuvent créer des solutions de logement qui améliorent les vies tout en respectant les droits des personnes et l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Informe de la ONU destaca soluciones exitosas para viviendas que mejoran vidas y comunidades
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En todo el mundo, miles de millones de personas no tienen hogares adecuados. Muchas viven en asentamientos informales o barrios marginales, enfrentando condiciones difíciles e inseguridad. La ONU publicó un nuevo informe en un foro urbano global para buscar soluciones a esta crisis de vivienda.

**Qué pasó** – El informe de ONU-Hábitat, presentado en el 13º Foro Mundial de Ciudades en Bakú, Azerbaiyán, muestra ejemplos de países como Tailandia, Brasil, Jordania, Alemania y Tanzania. Estos ejemplos demuestran cómo comunidades y gobiernos trabajan juntos para mejorar las viviendas sin desalojar a las personas. Los proyectos incluyen mejorar las favelas en Brasil, crear parques resistentes al clima en Jordania y apoyar mejoras de vivienda dirigidas por la comunidad en Tailandia.

**Impacto** – Este informe es importante porque presenta soluciones prácticas que funcionan en diferentes lugares y culturas. También destaca que la vivienda es un derecho humano, no solo un producto para comprar o vender. El informe conecta la mejora de viviendas con la lucha contra el cambio climático y el apoyo a grupos vulnerables como los refugiados. Cuestiona las viejas ideas de desalojos y demolición de barrios, enfocándose en mejorar las viviendas donde las personas ya viven.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU quiere que gobiernos y ciudades usen estos ejemplos para crear planes de vivienda a largo plazo basados en derechos humanos y resiliencia climática. Anima a una mayor cooperación entre las comunidades locales y grupos internacionales para hacer viviendas seguras, asequibles e inclusivas para todos.

**Resumen en una frase** – El informe de la ONU muestra que trabajando junto a las comunidades, los países pueden crear soluciones de vivienda que mejoran vidas respetando los derechos de las personas y el medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of diverse, interconnected miniature neighborhoods representing favelas, informal settlements, and urban parks, linked by flowing ribbons symbolizing community partnership and UN support; the scene includes symbolic elements like sturdy houses with solar panels, communal gathering spaces, and gentle hands cradling the neighborhoods from below, all rendered in natural, soft earth tones and muted blues to evoke hope, resilience, and inclusive urban growth.

articles.title: Norway: Court Blocks Activist’s Extradition to Greece
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Tommy Olsen.
                    © 2021 Daniel Berg Fosseng/TV 2 
          
    


  
(Athens, May 19, 2026) – The decision by a Norwegian appeals court on May 15, 2026, to block the extradition of a human rights defender, Tommy Olsen, to Greece is a victory for human rights, Human Rights Watch said today. A district court had initially approved the extradition request on March 16, but Olsen filed an appeal.The Hålogaland Court of Appeal unanimously recognized that the acts described by Greek authorities in its extradition request do not constitute criminal offenses under Norwegian law, and that his extradition would risk violating his right to freedom of expression under article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court concluded that Olsen’s actions were lawful acts protected by international treaties that bind both Norway and Greece.“The court’s decision not to extradite Tommy Olsen is a victory for the work of human rights defenders, and a direct rebuff to Greece’s attempt to export its crackdown on dissent,” said Eva Cossé, senior Europe researcher at Human Rights Watch. “Greece should now revoke the warrant and drop all charges against Olsen and his codefendant Panayote Dimitras.”Olsen, founder of the nongovernmental group Aegean Boat Report, was targeted in 2023 by Greek authorities with unfounded charges linked to his work documenting illegal pushbacks of asylum seekers and migrants at Greece’s borders. He is being prosecuted by Greek authorities alongside a Greek human rights defender, Panayote Dimitras, of Greek Helsinki Monitor. Greek authorities should drop all charges against Olsen and Dimitras and stop weaponizing criminal law to silence the country’s critics, Human Rights Watch said.The case against Olsen and Dimitras is part of a wider pattern of misuse of criminal law to harass activists defending the rights of migrants. In January, a Greek court in Lesbos acquitted 24 humanitarian workers of the felony charges against them after a seven-year ordeal, during which some had spent periods in detention for their peaceful activism. The European Parliament described it as the “largest case of criminalization of solidarity in Europe.”Greek authorities recently passed legislation that makes it easier to criminalize civil society organizations involved in aiding migrants and asylum seekers.The United Nations Special Rapporteur on the situation of human right defenders, Mary Lawlor, expressed deep concern on March 19 about Olsen’s arrest, stating that these charges appear to be “in direct retaliation” for Olsen’s work and to form part of a “long-standing and well-documented repression” of rights defenders in Greece.Extraditions under the European Arrest Warrant from one European Union state to another are largely automatic (with similar rules for Norway, a non-EU state), but the Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled that they can be delayed or halted on human rights grounds. As long as Olsen remains subject to an extradition request under the warrant, he will face a risk of arrest and extradition to Greece if he travels to any European Union state.“Olsen remains at risk of politically motivated prosecution and extradition as long as Greece’s European Arrest Warrant remains in force,” Cossé said. “Greece should revoke the warrant, and other states should make clear they will respond to it as the Norwegian court has done.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Tommy Olsen. © 2021 Daniel Berg Fosseng/TV 2

(Athens, May 19, 2026) – The decision by a Norwegian appeals court on May 15, 2026, to block the extradition of a human rights defender, Tommy Olsen, to Greece is a victory for human rights, Human Rights Watch said today. A district court had initially approved the extradition request on March 16, but Olsen filed an appeal.

The Hålogaland Court of Appeal unanimously recognized that the acts described by Greek authorities in its extradition request do not constitute criminal offenses under Norwegian law, and that his extradition would risk violating his right to freedom of expression under article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court concluded that Olsen’s actions were lawful acts protected by international treaties that bind both Norway and Greece.

“The court’s decision not to extradite Tommy Olsen is a victory for the work of human rights defenders, and a direct rebuff to Greece’s attempt to export its crackdown on dissent,” said Eva Cossé, senior Europe researcher at Human Rights Watch. “Greece should now revoke the warrant and drop all charges against Olsen and his codefendant Panayote Dimitras.”

Olsen, founder of the nongovernmental group Aegean Boat Report, was targeted in 2023 by Greek authorities with unfounded charges linked to his work documenting illegal pushbacks of asylum seekers and migrants at Greece’s borders. He is being prosecuted by Greek authorities alongside a Greek human rights defender, Panayote Dimitras, of Greek Helsinki Monitor. Greek authorities should drop all charges against Olsen and Dimitras and stop weaponizing criminal law to silence the country’s critics, Human Rights Watch said.

The case against Olsen and Dimitras is part of a wider pattern of misuse of criminal law to harass activists defending the rights of migrants. In January, a Greek court in Lesbos acquitted 24 humanitarian workers of the felony charges against them after a seven-year ordeal, during which some had spent periods in detention for their peaceful activism. The European Parliament described it as the “largest case of criminalization of solidarity in Europe.”

Greek authorities recently passed legislation that makes it easier to criminalize civil society organizations involved in aiding migrants and asylum seekers.

The United Nations Special Rapporteur on the situation of human right defenders, Mary Lawlor, expressed deep concern on March 19 about Olsen’s arrest, stating that these charges appear to be “in direct retaliation” for Olsen’s work and to form part of a “long-standing and well-documented repression” of rights defenders in Greece.

Extraditions under the European Arrest Warrant from one European Union state to another are largely automatic (with similar rules for Norway, a non-EU state), but the Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled that they can be delayed or halted on human rights grounds. As long as Olsen remains subject to an extradition request under the warrant, he will face a risk of arrest and extradition to Greece if he travels to any European Union state.

“Olsen remains at risk of politically motivated prosecution and extradition as long as Greece’s European Arrest Warrant remains in force,” Cossé said. “Greece should revoke the warrant, and other states should make clear they will respond to it as the Norwegian court has done.”
articles.reasoning: The Norwegian court's decision to block the extradition of a human rights defender, Tommy Olsen, protects freedom of expression and sets a precedent against misuse of criminal law to silence activists. This ruling has broad significance for human rights defenders across Europe and highlights judicial recognition of international human rights protections.
articles.personality_title: Norwegian court blocks extradition of human rights defender to Greece
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Tommy Olsen is a human rights defender who reports on the treatment of migrants at Greece’s borders. Greek authorities accused him of crimes related to his work, but these charges are seen by many as unfair and politically motivated.

**What happened** – On May 15, 2026, a Norwegian appeals court decided not to send Tommy Olsen back to Greece. The court said the actions he took are legal under Norwegian law and protected by international human rights agreements. This overturned an earlier decision that had allowed his extradition.

**Impact** – This decision protects Olsen’s right to free speech and shows that courts can stop countries from using legal charges to silence human rights defenders. It also challenges Greece’s efforts to punish activists who help migrants. The ruling is important because it supports activists across Europe and confirms that international human rights rules must be respected.

**What’s next step** – Olsen still faces risks because Greece has an active European Arrest Warrant for him. Human Rights Watch and others want Greece to cancel this warrant and drop all charges against Olsen and his co-defendant, Panayote Dimitras. Other countries are also expected to follow Norway’s example and protect human rights defenders from unfair extradition.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Norway’s court stopped the extradition of Tommy Olsen, safeguarding his freedom and setting a legal example against misuse of criminal law to punish human rights work.
articles.personality_title_fr: La cour norvégienne bloque l'extradition d'un défenseur des droits humains vers la Grèce
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Tommy Olsen est un défenseur des droits humains qui rapporte le traitement des migrants aux frontières grecques. Les autorités grecques l'ont accusé de crimes liés à son travail, mais ces accusations sont considérées par beaucoup comme injustes et politiquement motivées.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 15 mai 2026, une cour d'appel norvégienne a décidé de ne pas renvoyer Tommy Olsen en Grèce. La cour a estimé que ses actions sont légales selon la loi norvégienne et protégées par des accords internationaux sur les droits humains. Cette décision annule une précédente autorisation d'extradition.

**Impact** – Cette décision protège le droit d'Olsen à la liberté d'expression et montre que les tribunaux peuvent empêcher des pays d'utiliser la loi pour faire taire les défenseurs des droits humains. Elle remet en cause les efforts de la Grèce pour punir les militants aidant les migrants. Ce jugement est important car il soutient les activistes en Europe et confirme le respect des règles internationales sur les droits humains.

**Prochaine étape** – Olsen reste en danger car la Grèce a un mandat d'arrêt européen actif contre lui. Human Rights Watch et d'autres demandent à la Grèce d'annuler ce mandat et de retirer toutes les charges contre Olsen et son coaccusé Panayote Dimitras. D'autres pays devraient aussi suivre l'exemple de la Norvège pour protéger les défenseurs des droits humains contre des extraditions injustes.

**Une phrase clé** – La cour norvégienne a stoppé l'extradition de Tommy Olsen, protégeant sa liberté et établissant un exemple juridique contre l'usage abusif de la loi pour punir le travail des défenseurs des droits humains.
articles.personality_title_es: Tribunal noruego bloque la extradición de un defensor de derechos humanos a Grecia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Tommy Olsen es un defensor de los derechos humanos que informa sobre el trato a los migrantes en las fronteras de Grecia. Las autoridades griegas lo acusaron de delitos relacionados con su trabajo, pero muchos consideran que estas acusaciones son injustas y motivadas políticamente.

**Qué pasó** – El 15 de mayo de 2026, un tribunal de apelación noruego decidió no enviar a Tommy Olsen de regreso a Grecia. El tribunal dijo que sus acciones son legales bajo la ley noruega y están protegidas por acuerdos internacionales de derechos humanos. Esto revocó una decisión anterior que permitía su extradición.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión protege el derecho de Olsen a la libertad de expresión y demuestra que los tribunales pueden impedir que los países usen cargos legales para silenciar a defensores de derechos humanos. También desafía los esfuerzos de Grecia para castigar a activistas que ayudan a migrantes. La sentencia es importante porque apoya a activistas en toda Europa y confirma que se deben respetar las normas internacionales de derechos humanos.

**Próximo paso** – Olsen aún corre riesgos porque Grecia tiene una orden de arresto europea activa contra él. Human Rights Watch y otros piden a Grecia que cancele esta orden y retire todos los cargos contra Olsen y su coacusado Panayote Dimitras. Se espera que otros países sigan el ejemplo de Noruega y protejan a los defensores de derechos humanos de extradiciones injustas.

**Una frase clave** – El tribunal noruego detuvo la extradición de Tommy Olsen, protegiendo su libertad y estableciendo un ejemplo legal contra el uso indebido de la ley para castigar el trabajo de los defensores de derechos humanos.
articles.image_prompt: A serene harbor scene at dawn with a sturdy wooden boat named “Aegean Boat Report” gently anchored near a peaceful shore, surrounded by calm waters reflecting soft pastel hues, while a pair of open, unchained handcuffs rest on the dock beside a folded European Convention on Human Rights document symbolized by a glowing parchment, all rendered in warm, natural tones with delicate brushstrokes conveying hope and justice.

articles.title: California sends newborns home with a month of free diapers
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM New babies go through eight to ten diapers a day, and diapers run about $100 a month. For families already stretched, that bill arrives before they’ve slept. Some parents leave diapers on too long or reuse disposables, which leads to rashes and infections. This particular problem often starts the […]
The post California sends newborns home with a month of free diapers first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

New babies go through eight to ten diapers a day, and diapers run about $100 a month. For families already stretched, that bill arrives before they’ve slept. Some parents leave diapers on too long or reuse disposables, which leads to rashes and infections. This particular problem often starts the day they leave the hospital or even earlier. Luckily, California is trying to get ahead of it before they leave the hospital.

400 diapers, no paperwork required

Under a new program from Governor Gavin Newsom, every family delivering at a participating hospital goes home with 400 diapers in newborn and infant sizes, covering babies up to 14 pounds (6 kilograms). About five weeks’ worth. No income check or enrollment form needed.

The program, Golden State Start, runs through a partnership with the non-profit Baby2Baby, which built its own manufacturing line to produce diapers at 80 percent below retail. “California is once again setting the standard of what it means to show up for mothers and babies,” said co-CEO Norah Weinstein.

Starting where the need is greatest

In year one, Golden State Start will run at 65 to 75 hospitals covering about a quarter of California births, with priority given to facilities that largely serve low-income patients. It will expand from there, though the state hasn’t set a specific timeline.

The state put $7.4 million toward it in last year’s budget. This year’s proposal adds $12.5 million to carry it through June 2027.

The first of its kind at discharge

Two other states have moved on diapers, but differently. Tennessee lets Medicaid-enrolled families pick up 100 diapers a month at pharmacies for children under two. Delaware has a pilot providing up to 80 diapers and one pack of wipes per week in the first twelve weeks, extended in 2024. Neither delivers at discharge to all families regardless of income.

California Medicaid covers diapers only for enrollees aged five and older with a documented medical need. Golden State Start is outside that system entirely.

“The first days at home with a newborn should be focused on the love, connection, and joy of an expanded family, not stress about affording diapers,” said state health secretary Kim Johnson. “This program helps ensure families can begin that journey with greater stability and peace of mind.”

Newsom put it alongside earlier moves: free school meals, universal preschool. “Every baby born in California deserves a healthy start in life,” he said, “and that means making sure parents have the basics they need from day one.”

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a new California program providing every family with newborns a free supply of diapers at hospital discharge, addressing a significant financial and health burden for families, especially low-income ones. This initiative has broad societal impact by improving infant health and family stability, with substantial state funding and plans for expansion. It is a focused, concrete policy action with meaningful benefits for many families.
articles.personality_title: California gives new parents a month of free diapers at hospital discharge
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Newborn babies usually need eight to ten diapers a day, which can cost around $100 a month. For many families, especially those with less money, buying diapers can be a big challenge. Sometimes parents keep diapers on too long or reuse them, causing rashes and infections.

**What happened** – California started a new program called Golden State Start. Since 2023, every family with a newborn at a participating hospital receives 400 free diapers, enough for about five weeks. The diapers come in newborn and infant sizes and are given without any paperwork or income checks. The program works with Baby2Baby, a nonprofit that makes diapers at much lower costs.

**Impact** – This is the first program in the U.S. to give diapers to all families right when they leave the hospital, not just those who qualify for government aid. It helps reduce stress for new parents and protects babies from health problems linked to diaper shortages. The program started in 65 to 75 hospitals, mostly serving families with low incomes, and has strong state funding to continue expanding.

**What's next step** – California plans to grow the program to more hospitals, aiming to cover more families over time. The state budget includes millions of dollars to keep the program running through 2027, showing a serious commitment to supporting new parents.

**One-sentence takeaway** – California’s Golden State Start program provides free diapers to all newborn families at hospital discharge, easing costs and improving baby health from day one.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Californie offre un mois de couches gratuites aux nouveaux parents à la sortie de l’hôpital
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les nouveau-nés utilisent généralement huit à dix couches par jour, ce qui coûte environ 100 dollars par mois. Pour de nombreuses familles, surtout celles avec moins d’argent, acheter des couches peut être un vrai défi. Parfois, les parents gardent les couches trop longtemps ou les réutilisent, ce qui cause des irritations et des infections.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La Californie a lancé un nouveau programme appelé Golden State Start. Depuis 2023, chaque famille avec un nouveau-né dans un hôpital participant reçoit 400 couches gratuites, soit environ cinq semaines d’utilisation. Les couches sont en tailles nouveau-né et bébé, distribuées sans paperasse ni vérification de revenu. Le programme travaille avec Baby2Baby, une association qui fabrique des couches à coût réduit.

**Impact** – C’est le premier programme aux États-Unis à offrir des couches à toutes les familles dès leur sortie de l’hôpital, pas seulement à celles aidées par l’État. Cela réduit le stress des nouveaux parents et protège les bébés des problèmes de santé liés au manque de couches. Le programme a commencé dans 65 à 75 hôpitaux, surtout ceux qui servent des familles à faibles revenus, avec un financement public important.

**Prochaine étape** – La Californie prévoit d’étendre le programme à plus d’hôpitaux pour toucher davantage de familles. Le budget de l’État prévoit des millions de dollars pour continuer le programme jusqu’en 2027, montrant un engagement sérieux.

**En une phrase** – Le programme Golden State Start de Californie offre des couches gratuites à toutes les familles de nouveau-nés à la sortie de l’hôpital, réduisant les coûts et améliorant la santé des bébés dès le premier jour.
articles.personality_title_es: California entrega un mes de pañales gratis a familias con recién nacidos al salir del hospital
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los bebés recién nacidos usan entre ocho y diez pañales al día, lo que puede costar alrededor de 100 dólares al mes. Para muchas familias, especialmente las que tienen menos dinero, comprar pañales es un gran desafío. A veces, los padres dejan los pañales puestos demasiado tiempo o los reutilizan, causando irritaciones e infecciones.

**Qué pasó** – California inició un nuevo programa llamado Golden State Start. Desde 2023, todas las familias con un recién nacido en un hospital participante reciben 400 pañales gratis, suficientes para unas cinco semanas. Los pañales vienen en tallas para recién nacidos y bebés, y se entregan sin trámites ni comprobación de ingresos. El programa trabaja con Baby2Baby, una organización que fabrica pañales a un costo mucho menor.

**Impacto** – Este es el primer programa en Estados Unidos que da pañales a todas las familias justo al salir del hospital, no solo a quienes reciben ayuda del gobierno. Ayuda a reducir el estrés de los nuevos padres y protege a los bebés de problemas de salud relacionados con la falta de pañales. El programa comenzó en 65 a 75 hospitales, principalmente en áreas con familias de bajos ingresos, y cuenta con un fuerte financiamiento estatal.

**Próximo paso** – California planea expandir el programa a más hospitales para alcanzar a más familias. El presupuesto estatal incluye millones de dólares para mantener el programa hasta 2027, mostrando un compromiso serio.

**Resumen en una frase** – El programa Golden State Start de California entrega pañales gratis a todas las familias con recién nacidos al salir del hospital, aliviando costos y mejorando la salud de los bebés desde el primer día.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy nursery scene featuring a soft wooden cradle gently cradling a bundle of glowing, stylized diapers arranged like protective blankets, surrounded by warm, muted earth tones and a subtle golden light symbolizing care and support, with a simple hospital building silhouette in the background to represent the program’s origin.
